occurs (the model weight buffer is statically allocated at boot per
the kernel-context constraint).

\subsection{4-invariant regression gate}
\label{sec:results-kernel-gate}

A Makefile target \texttt{make test-intllm-kernel-path} exercises the
inference flow inside QEMU on every commit, gating mechanical
stability of the path as an artifact of the build. The gate uses
QEMU with the FajarOS Nova ISO booted from CD-ROM
(\texttt{-boot order=d}) and an attached NVMe disk image carrying a
tiny synthetic v9 model checkpoint, waits for the shell prompt, sends
\texttt{model-load nvme 0} and \texttt{ask hello} commands via the
serial console, captures the serial-console output to a log file, and
checks four invariants:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{No fault markers.} The serial log contains neither
\texttt{EXC:} (CPU exception) nor \texttt{PANIC:} (kernel panic)
markers; the inference path completes without triggering the
kernel's mechanical-stability fail-safes.

\item \textbf{v9 header parsed.} The log contains
\texttt{[OK] Model header loaded}, confirming
\texttt{mdl\_parse\_header} accepted the v9 magic and dispatched the
checkpoint to the \texttt{fjm\_v9.fj} parser branch.

\item \textbf{\texttt{cmd\_ask} dispatched to
\texttt{tfm\_mf\_generate}.} A serial-log marker emitted from inside
\texttt{cmd\_ask}'s dispatch logic confirms the v9 routing decision
took the IntLLM kernel-path rather than the legacy v8 RWKV path.

\item \textbf{Shell recovered after \texttt{ask}.} The log contains a
return to the \texttt{nova>} prompt after the inference completes,
confirming the kernel state was not corrupted by the inference run
and the shell remains responsive.
\end{enumerate}

The gate explicitly does NOT verify token-level coherence quality of
the generated output: the synthetic test model used by the regression
gate is a tiny path-verification fixture, not a quality-evaluation
model. Generation-quality verification is the job of the offline
Phase~D Mini/Base/Medium training results
(Section~\ref{sec:results-scaling}); the kernel-path gate verifies
\emph{the path itself works}, not \emph{the model is good}.

\subsection{Binary footprint and budget}
\label{sec:results-kernel-budget}

Table~\ref{tab:kernel-binary} reports the static binary footprint of
the FajarOS Nova~v3.9.0 build artifact set, against the
$\leq 16$~MB total-budget constraint of contribution~C3.

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{FajarOS Nova~v3.9.0 binary footprint. The \emph{kernel ELF} is
the unstripped output of the Fajar Lang LLVM backend
(\texttt{build/fajaros-llvm.elf}); the \emph{ISO} adds GRUB and
boot-stage loaders for QEMU/UEFI booting. The \emph{model} column is
the ternary checkpoint loaded at runtime via NVMe (not part of the
kernel binary; loaded into a static @kernel buffer at
\texttt{model-load} time). Total budget is kernel~+~model~$\leq 16$~MB
per the kernel-context constraint of Section~\ref{sec:arch}.}
\label{tab:kernel-binary}
\begin{tabular}{lr}
\toprule
Component                              & Size \\
\midrule
FajarOS Nova v3.9.0 kernel ELF (unstripped)   & 1.66~MB \\
\quad of which: \texttt{matmulfree.fj} kernel ops & $\sim$0.18~MB \\
\quad of which: \texttt{fjm\_v9.fj} parser    & $\sim$0.04~MB \\
\quad of which: \texttt{tfm\_matmulfree.fj} forward & $\sim$0.06~MB \\
\quad of which: rest of FajarOS Nova kernel   & $\sim$1.38~MB \\
ISO image (with GRUB + boot loaders)          & 13.82~MB \\
\midrule
Phase~D Medium ternary model (\texttt{.fjm v9}) & $\sim$14.7~MB$^*$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\\[2pt]
{\footnotesize $^*$~Estimated as $74.5$~M params $\times 1.58$~bits$/$8 = 14.71~MB
ternary-packed weights, plus $\sim$0.4~MB of full-precision $\beta$
scales and embedding/lm\_head matrices retained at higher precision in
the v9 export format. Direct measurement of an exported Medium model
file is deferred to the camera-ready version (Path A outline §5
``MUST-HAVE before submission'' checklist).}
\end{table}

The kernel ELF at 1.66~MB plus the Phase~D Medium ternary model at
$\sim$14.7~MB sums to $\sim$16.36~MB, slightly exceeding the
$\leq 16$~MB design budget at the Medium scale. Two of three
deployment paths satisfy the budget: (a)~the Mini-scale model
($\sim$4.3~MB ternary) plus kernel ELF totals $\sim$6.0~MB, well
under budget; (b)~the Base-scale model ($\sim$9.2~MB ternary) plus
kernel ELF totals $\sim$10.9~MB. Medium-scale deployment requires
either kernel-side stripping (currently disabled for debugging) or
v9-format compression (not yet implemented); we report the unmodified
Medium total honestly and treat the marginal overrun as a
calibration target for v9-format follow-up work.

Figure~\ref{fig:kernel-budget} (deferred to camera-ready) plots the
budget breakdown stacked by component.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/kernel_budget.pdf}
\caption{FajarOS Nova~v3.9.0 binary budget breakdown by component.
Source: \texttt{build/} artifact inspection; per-module sizes from
ELF section table.}
\label{fig:kernel-budget}
\end{figure}

\label{sec:results-kernel-runtime}

Table~\ref{tab:kernel-runtime} reports the runtime metrics for the

in Section~\ref{sec:results-kernel-budget} are direct artifact

the target hardware (RTX~4090~Laptop CPU host running QEMU; bare

the V31 cycle does not have access to).

The 4-invariant kernel-path gate of
Section~\ref{sec:results-kernel-gate} is sufficient for the
\emph{path-verification} claim of contribution~C3 — the inference
runs end-to-end inside @kernel context without violating the
compile-time safety invariants of Section~\ref{sec:safety}. Runtime
performance numbers are an additional axis on which we make no
camera-ready claim until measurement is complete; the paper is
explicit that C3's contribution is the \emph{architectural integration},
not a benchmark beat against userspace inference engines like
\texttt{llama.cpp} or \texttt{Bitnet.cpp}~\citep{xu2025bitnet} that
operate in fundamentally different deployment regimes.

\subsection{Honest scope of contribution C3}
\label{sec:results-kernel-discuss}

What the kernel-path establishes:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item Phase~D ternary checkpoints can be loaded and inferenced
\emph{without leaving the operating-system kernel address space} on
a real (FajarOS Nova) operating system.
\item The four mechanical-stability invariants pass on every CI run,
gating regressions to the inference path on every commit.
\item The static binary footprint at the Mini and Base scales fits
the $\leq 16$~MB design budget; Medium overruns marginally and
identifies v9-format compression as a follow-up calibration target.
\end{enumerate}

What the kernel-path does NOT establish:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item Token-level coherence-quality verification — that is the job of
Section~\ref{sec:results-scaling} and Section~\ref{sec:bilingual-q5},
not the kernel-path gate.
\item Bare-metal performance numbers (tok/s, watts) — pending
measurement; deferred to a future deployment-platform partner.
\item Production-grade hardening of the FajarOS Nova kernel itself —
the kernel passes its own 35-test regression suite (per the FajarOS
Nova v3.9.0 release notes) but is research-grade, not formally
verified in the seL4~\citep{klein2009sel4} sense.
\end{enumerate}

This honest delimitation of C3 is consistent with the
research-integrity rules R3 and R6 of
Section~\ref{sec:method-integrity}: we report what we have measured
mechanically, leave gaps explicit, and avoid implying claims for
which we lack the measurement infrastructure.

% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
% §9 Compiler-Verified Safety (C5) — DRAFTED THIS COMMIT
% (Path A Week 3 sub-step 2)
% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\section{Compiler-Verified Safety (C5)}
\label{sec:safety}

The Fajar Lang compiler enforces a four-context type system that
rules out kernel-context safety violations at compile time. The
\texttt{@kernel}, \texttt{@device}, \texttt{@safe}, and
\texttt{@unsafe} annotations partition functions and call sites into
deployment domains; the analyzer pass between parser and code
generator validates that no function-call edge crosses a domain
boundary in a way that would violate the target deployment's
invariants. This is the contribution that distinguishes IntLLM from
``ternary in userspace'' deployments
\citep{ma2024era,xu2025bitnet}: the kernel-context invariants of
Section~\ref{sec:results-kernel} are mechanically enforced by the
compiler rather than relied on as a documentation-grade convention.

\subsection{The four-context type system}
\label{sec:safety-contexts}

Every function in a Fajar Lang program carries exactly one of four
context annotations, with the following operational meaning:

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item \texttt{@safe} (default if no annotation specified):
unrestricted within the safe-by-default subset; no hardware access,
no raw pointers, no kernel privileged operations, no direct tensor
construction. Userspace business-logic code lives here.

\item \texttt{@kernel}: kernel-context. Allowed: stack allocation,
raw pointer dereference, page-map and IRQ-register intrinsics
(\texttt{alloc!()}, \texttt{irq\_register!()}, etc.), syscalls
\emph{into} the kernel from \texttt{@kernel} functions. Forbidden:
heap allocation (\texttt{String}, \texttt{Vec}), tensor construction
(\texttt{zeros}, \texttt{ones}, \texttt{relu}, etc.), calls to
\texttt{@device}-marked functions.

\item \texttt{@device}: device / accelerator context (GPU, NPU,
embedded inference accelerator). Allowed: tensor operations and
device-side intrinsics. Forbidden: raw pointer dereference outside
explicit \texttt{@unsafe} blocks, hardware-register intrinsics
(\texttt{irq\_register!}, \texttt{port\_write!}, \texttt{map\_page!}),
calls to \texttt{@kernel}-marked functions.

\item \texttt{@unsafe}: full access; bypasses all of the above
restrictions. Used only at deliberate intent points (FFI shims,
specialized kernel modules) and clearly delimited.
\end{itemize}

Table~\ref{tab:safety-matrix} reproduces the operation-by-context
matrix from the Fajar Lang specification (CLAUDE.md~§5.3) showing
which operations are allowed in each context. The
type-system-enforcement of this matrix is what gives the kernel-LLM
deployment of Section~\ref{sec:results-kernel} its safety guarantees:
\textbf{any IntLLM kernel-path code that violates the matrix fails to
compile, before a kernel binary is ever produced.}

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{Fajar Lang context-operation matrix. \texttt{OK}~=~allowed,
\texttt{ERROR~XXNNN}~=~rejected with the indicated error code. The
\texttt{@safe} column is the most restrictive subset; \texttt{@unsafe}
allows all operations; \texttt{@kernel} and \texttt{@device} are the
two specialized contexts that the IntLLM kernel-path
(Section~\ref{sec:results-kernel}) and the training-time
\texttt{@device} tensor operations (Section~\ref{sec:method}) live
in respectively. Source: CLAUDE.md~§5.3, ERROR\_CODES.md~§5.}
\label{tab:safety-matrix}
\begin{tabular}{lcccc}
\toprule
Operation                & \texttt{@safe} & \texttt{@kernel} & \texttt{@device} & \texttt{@unsafe} \\
\midrule
\texttt{let x = 42}             & OK       & OK       & OK       & OK \\
\texttt{String::new()}          & OK       & KE001    & OK       & OK \\
\texttt{zeros(3,4)} / \texttt{relu()}        & TE       & KE002    & OK       & OK \\
\texttt{alloc!(4096)}                        & ERROR    & OK       & DE002    & OK \\
\texttt{*mut T} dereference                  & ERROR    & OK       & DE001    & OK \\
\texttt{irq\_register!()}                    & ERROR    & OK       & DE002    & OK \\
Call \texttt{@device fn}                     & OK       & KE003    & OK       & OK \\
Call \texttt{@kernel fn}                     & OK       & OK       & DE002    & OK \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

The bottom two rows are particularly load-bearing: a
\texttt{@kernel} function calling a \texttt{@device} function would
cross a deployment-domain boundary (kernel-resident code suddenly
needing tensor runtime support), and conversely a \texttt{@device}
function calling a \texttt{@kernel} function would invert the
privileged/unprivileged direction. Both are mechanically rejected.

\subsection{Compile-time enforcement — error catalog}
\label{sec:safety-errors}

Table~\ref{tab:kernel-errors} reproduces the kernel- and
device-context error codes that the Fajar Lang compiler emits when
the matrix of Table~\ref{tab:safety-matrix} is violated. Each error
code corresponds to a specific check in the analyzer pass between
the parser and the code generator, runs at compile time, and is a
hard error (not a warning).

\begin{table}[t]
\centering
\small
\caption{Kernel and Device context error codes from
\texttt{docs/ERROR\_CODES.md} \textsection5.1-5.2. Total: 4 kernel
errors (KE001-KE004) + 3 device errors (DE001-DE003).}
\label{tab:kernel-errors}
\begin{tabular}{llp{4cm}}
\toprule
Code  & Name                 & Trigger example \\
\midrule
KE001 & HeapAllocInKernel    & \texttt{String::new()}, \texttt{Vec::new()} in \texttt{@kernel} \\
KE002 & TensorInKernel       & \texttt{zeros()}, \texttt{relu()}, \texttt{.backward()} in \texttt{@kernel} \\
KE003 & DeviceCallInKernel   & \texttt{@device fn} called from \texttt{@kernel} \\
KE004 & InvalidKernelOp      & Operations requiring heap (e.g. \texttt{format!()}) \\
DE001 & RawPointerInDevice   & \texttt{*mut T} dereference outside \texttt{@unsafe} block \\
DE002 & HardwareInDevice     & \texttt{irq\_register!}, \texttt{port\_write!}, \texttt{map\_page!} \\
DE003 & InvalidDeviceOp      & \texttt{asm!()}, raw-memory access in \texttt{@device} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

A representative compile-time output for KE001 (HeapAllocInKernel):

\begin{lstlisting}[basicstyle=\ttfamily\footnotesize]
error[KE001]: heap allocation not allowed
              in @kernel context
  --> kernel.fj:8:5
   |
7  | @kernel
8  | fn init_kernel() {
9  |     let s = String::new();
   |             ^^^^^^^^^^^^^ heap alloc
   |
   = note: @kernel operates without heap
   = help: use stack-allocated arrays or
           alloc!() for raw memory
\end{lstlisting}

The error includes the source-span pointer to the offending call
site, a note explaining the kernel-context invariant being violated,
and a help suggestion pointing at the kernel-safe alternative
(\texttt{alloc!()} for raw memory). All seven error codes follow the
same source-span + note + help template, satisfying the
diagnostic-quality bar of established systems languages
\citep{klein2009sel4}.

\subsection{Compilation pipeline}
\label{sec:safety-pipeline}

The four-context type system is enforced by the analyzer pass of
the Fajar Lang compiler, which sits between parsing and code
generation. Figure~\ref{fig:compiler-pipeline} (deferred to
camera-ready) plots the pipeline; the textual description here
suffices for the contribution claim.

\begin{figure}[t]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figures/compiler_pipeline.pdf}
\caption{Fajar Lang compilation pipeline. The context-check sub-pass
of the analyzer is the load-bearing C5 contribution: it traverses
the analyzed AST and rejects any call-site edge crossing the
context-operation matrix of Table~\ref{tab:safety-matrix}.}
\label{fig:compiler-pipeline}
\end{figure}

The context-check is implemented as a tree-walk over the analyzed
AST. For each function definition, it records the function's context
annotation (or assigns \texttt{@safe} as default); for each call site
inside that function, it checks whether the called function's
context is reachable from the calling function's context per the
matrix; for each operation expression, it checks whether the
operation is allowed in the calling context. Context violations
produce a hard compile error with the source span; the program does
not proceed to code generation.

\subsection{Bridge pattern for cross-domain code}
\label{sec:safety-bridge}

A common deployment shape combines kernel-side I/O (sensor read,
hardware register access) with device-side ML inference; the Fajar
Lang \texttt{@safe} subset serves as the glue layer that bridges the
two without violating either context's invariants:

\begin{lstlisting}[basicstyle=\ttfamily\footnotesize]
@kernel
fn read_sensor() -> [f32; 4] {
    // hardware-register reads here
    // ...
    return data
}

@device
fn infer(x: Tensor) -> Tensor {
    // tensor ops here
    return model.forward(x)
}

@safe
fn bridge() -> Action {
    let raw = read_sensor()
    let result = infer(
        Tensor::from_slice(raw))
    return Action::from_prediction(result)
}
\end{lstlisting}

The bridge function is \texttt{@safe} because it constructs a
\texttt{Tensor} (forbidden in \texttt{@kernel}) and reads sensor
data (the \texttt{read\_sensor} return type is a stack-allocated
array, not a raw pointer, so the data crosses the
\texttt{@kernel}~$\to$~\texttt{@safe} boundary cleanly). The
type system permits \texttt{@safe} to call both \texttt{@kernel}
and \texttt{@device} functions because the \texttt{@safe} caller
itself is not subject to either context's specialized invariants.
This bridge pattern is the canonical way to compose kernel-side I/O
with device-side ML in Fajar Lang programs; the IntLLM kernel-path of
Section~\ref{sec:results-kernel} uses it for the
\texttt{model-load}~/~\texttt{ask} shell command flow.

\subsection{Honest scope: what the type system does and does not guarantee}
\label{sec:safety-scope}

What C5 establishes:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item Compile-time rejection of code that would violate the
kernel-context invariants of Section~\ref{sec:results-kernel} (no
heap, no tensor, no \texttt{@device} call). Programs that pass the
analyzer are guaranteed to satisfy these invariants \emph{at the
language level} for any kernel-path code path.

\item Hard errors (not warnings) — programs with safety violations
do not produce kernel binaries. This rules out the failure mode where
a documentation-grade convention is silently violated by a future
contributor and only discovered at runtime.

\item Source-span + note + help diagnostic quality matching
established systems-language standards.
\end{enumerate}

What C5 does NOT establish:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item Formal-verification-grade correctness of the
\emph{implementation} of the analyzer itself. The seL4 kernel
\citep{klein2009sel4} extends compile-time invariants with
machine-checked proofs that the compiler implementation correctly
discharges those invariants; we do the former, not the latter. A
sufficiently determined contributor with patch access to the analyzer
could in principle disable a check.

\item Correctness of \texttt{@unsafe} blocks. The escape hatch
(intentional, used at FFI boundaries) explicitly opts out of the
type-system enforcement; kernel-binary safety inside \texttt{@unsafe}
relies on review-and-audit conventions, not type-system guarantees.

\item Runtime-detectable failures (memory corruption, use-after-free)
that require dynamic analysis. The type system rules out a class of
bugs (the seven error codes of Table~\ref{tab:kernel-errors}) but is
not a substitute for dynamic safety mechanisms (page-table protection,
canary values) that the FajarOS Nova kernel additionally provides.
\end{enumerate}

The C5 contribution sits at a specific point on the safety-mechanism
spectrum: stronger than userspace ML deployment's documentation-grade
conventions \citep{ma2024era, xu2025bitnet}, weaker than seL4's
machine-checked proofs \citep{klein2009sel4}. We claim the latter
position explicitly is out of scope; the former is what existing
ternary-LLM literature defaults to, and the type-system layer is a
material improvement over that default.

% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
% §10 Discussion and Future Work — DRAFTED THIS COMMIT
% (Path A Week 3 sub-step 3)
% ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
\section{Discussion and Future Work}
\label{sec:discussion}

\subsection{Synthesis of contributions}
\label{sec:discussion-synthesis}

The five contributions of this paper compose into a single
deployment-grade demonstration that is not addressed by any single
prior work along the union of relevant axes
(Section~\ref{sec:results-scaling-discuss},
Section~\ref{sec:bilingual-discuss}; full multi-axis positioning in
\texttt{docs/INTLLM\_RELATED\_WORK.md}~\textsection7.1):

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{C1.} The Phase~D scaling chain extends 1.58-bit ternary
training-from-scratch validation downward from BitNet~b1.58's
2~B-parameter regime~\citep{ma2024era} into the 22-74~M-parameter
range relevant for kernel-context deployment, with monotonic widening
gate margins ($0.118 \to 0.210 \to 0.279$~nat) over a $\sim$3.5$\times$
parameter range and a $1.94\times$ end-to-end PPL reduction.

\item \textbf{C2.} The Phase~E1 bilingual corpus v1.0 (25.67~B tokens
at 60:40 ID:EN, 0\%~synthetic, mechanical-consistency-gated) and the
Q5 Mini-scale baseline are the first fully-public bilingual ternary
LM resources at the 22~M-parameter scale, reproducible in $\sim$42
minutes on commodity laptop hardware.

\item \textbf{C3.} The FajarOS Nova~v3.9.0 IntLLM kernel-path
demonstrates a 1.58-bit ternary LM running entirely inside the
operating-system kernel address space, with a 4-invariant CI-gated
regression test and binary footprint within the
$\leq 16$~MB total budget at Mini and Base scales (Medium marginally
overruns; identifies v9-format compression as follow-up).

\item \textbf{C4.} Two outlier-handling features ported from the
post-hoc-quantization literature (balanced\_calib via SmoothQuant
\citep{xiao2022smoothquant}~/~AWQ \citep{lin2023awq};
Hadamard rotation via QuaRot \citep{ashkboos2024quarot}) FAIL their
mechanical adoption gates by margins of $-82.13$ and $-0.12$~nat
respectively. Three-cause attributions per failure converge on a
common pattern: \emph{post-hoc-literature precedents do not transfer
cleanly to training-from-scratch ternary at small scale}.

\item \textbf{C5.} The Fajar Lang four-context type system enforces
the kernel-context invariants of C3 at compile time via the seven
KE001-KE004~/~DE001-DE003 error codes, sitting at a specific point
on the safety-mechanism spectrum stronger than userspace ML
deployments' documentation-grade conventions and weaker (explicitly)
than seL4-class machine-checked proofs~\citep{klein2009sel4}.
\end{itemize}

The composition of these five — none of which is individually a
breakthrough — is what makes IntLLM a coherent
embedded-AI-plus-OS-integration system rather than an incremental
advance on any single axis.

\subsection{What this paper does NOT claim}
\label{sec:discussion-nonclaims}

The paper is deliberately scoped to what we can mechanically verify
on the available infrastructure:

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{Frontier benchmark performance.} IntLLM is 25-100$\times$
smaller than BitNet~b1.58~2B4T \citep{ma2024era} and orders of
magnitude smaller than frontier 7~B+ models. We do not claim parity
on multi-choice reasoning benchmarks; the 22-74~M scale is below the
emergence threshold for those skills per Pythia
\citep{biderman2023pythia} and Chinchilla \citep{hoffmann2022chinchilla}.

\item \textbf{Ternary parity with FP16 at the same parameter count.}
Pythia-160~M and SmolLM2-135~M~\citep{allal2024smollm2} achieve
materially better Wikitext word-PPL at comparable parameter counts.
The trade is parameter efficiency for kernel-deployable binary
footprint and integer-native arithmetic.

\item \textbf{Universal failure of outlier-handling features.} C4's
two negative results are at our specific regime (Mini-scale,
training-from-scratch, bilingual data); the literature's positive
post-hoc results at $\geq 7$~B FP16 are not refuted, and Phase~F.6
explicitly tests whether the post-hoc protocol recovers the literature
result on our trained Mini checkpoint.

\item \textbf{Production-grade FajarOS Nova.} The kernel passes its
own 35-test regression suite but is research-grade; no formal
verification, no hardened resource isolation beyond what the page-table
mechanisms provide, no defense-in-depth against malicious inputs.

\item \textbf{Bare-metal performance numbers.} Section~\ref{sec:results-kernel-runtime}
explicitly tags the tok/s and watts entries of
Table~\ref{tab:kernel-runtime} as PEND, deferred to a future
deployment-platform partner.
\end{enumerate}

These delimitations are not modesty — they are research-integrity
requirements per the methodology of
Section~\ref{sec:method-integrity} (R3, R6).

\subsection{Phase~F: immediate-next research agenda}
\label{sec:discussion-phasef}

The two C4 negative results, plus the three outlier-handling features
cut from the paper's scope under the Path~A decision
(Section~\ref{sec:negatives-future}), define a five-entry
research agenda committed in
\texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_F\_TAX\_VERTICAL\_ROADMAP.md}~v1.3 \textsection4.1:

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item \textbf{F.5 Post-training PTQ-style activation calibration.}
Replace the all-time-max running-max accumulator with a SmoothQuant
\citep{xiao2022smoothquant}-style held-out post-training calibration
on a fixed batch from the Q5-trained Mini checkpoint. Tests whether
the balanced\_calib failure was about WHEN calibration happens
(during-training accumulator) rather than WHAT calibration is computed
(per-channel scale). Cheapest of the five; entry condition is
sub-day smoke showing $\geq +10\%$ MSE reduction on outlier channels.

\item \textbf{F.6 Post-hoc QuaRot on Q5-trained Mini checkpoint —
NOW RECOMMENDED.} The Phase~F.6.1 prerequisite measurement
(Section~\ref{sec:negatives-had} cause~2) ran POST the V31 cycle and
found very strong outlier concentration on \texttt{o\_proj} inputs
(mean $51\times$ max-to-mean ratio, max $421\times$), far above the
$\geq 3\times$ threshold the F.6 entry condition requires. Post-hoc
QuaRot \citep{ashkboos2024quarot} on the Q5-trained checkpoint —
load, fuse Hadamard into adjacent weights via algebraic identity,
re-evaluate without re-training — is therefore RECOMMENDED rather
than gated. Whether the published positive results transfer to our
HGRN-Bit setting at this small scale becomes the next concrete
empirical question.

\item \textbf{F.7 FP8 (E4M3) mixed-precision for \texttt{lm\_head}~+~\texttt{attn.W\_o}.}
Original Phase~E2.2 sub-feature, cut to Phase~F under Path~A. Tests
whether structurally-different (mixed-precision rather than
outlier-handling) features behave differently than F.5/F.6/E2.4/E2.1.
Hardware compatibility check: \texttt{torch.float8\_e4m3fn}
availability on RTX~4090~Laptop pinned PyTorch version is the F.7.0
gate before F.7.1+ commits resources.

\item \textbf{F.8 FP16 distillation during QAT.} Original Phase~E2.3,
cut to Phase~F. Teacher~=~Phase~D Medium FP32 master (per Q3 closure,
viable via \texttt{model.half()}); negative-transfer risk on
ID-side samples (English-only teacher, bilingual student) addressed
by sampling-aware distill-weight design.

\item \textbf{F.9 Language-conditioned design.} Original Phase~E2.5,
cut to Phase~F. Re-evaluate Option~(a)/(b)/(c) selection given F.5-F.8
empirical evidence; if one of F.5-F.8 demonstrates that Phase~E-class
features can help under our regime, F.9 carries the explicit
responsibility to close the $1.77\times \to {\leq}1.5\times$
coherence-ratio headroom the Q5 baseline left open.
\end{itemize}

The five-entry agenda is paced for V32-cycle execution post-this-paper-acceptance;
estimated effort is 4-6 weeks human + $\sim$10-15~h GPU per
sub-feature.

\subsection{Longer-term roadmap}
\label{sec:discussion-longer-term}

Three longer-horizon items are acknowledged but out-of-scope for this
paper: \textbf{(i)} Phase~E3 bilingual scale-up to Base and Medium —
the full Mini~$\to$~Base~$\to$~Medium chain on the Phase~E1 corpus
rather than SlimPajama, $\sim$30-50~hours GPU; deferred until paper
acceptance. \textbf{(ii)} Phase~D Stretch (15~B tokens, fourth
scaling-chain point) — $\sim$17-day continuous-GPU laptop budget;
not required for the C1 claim. \textbf{(iii)} Tier~3 Phase~F.1-F.4
tax-vertical TaxPrime fine-tuning, deferred per
\texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_F\_TAX\_VERTICAL\_ROADMAP.md}~v1.3 \textsection F.1-F.4
on founder-bandwidth + NDA constraints; gates on this paper's
acceptance.

\subsection{Methodological reflection: Path~A as a methodology contribution}
\label{sec:discussion-methodology}

The decision to STOP Phase~E2 after two ablations both FAILED, rather
than push through three more ablations to fill the original
five-feature combined-ablation table, is itself a methodology choice
this paper claims as part of contribution~C4. The reasoning chain
(\texttt{FJQ\_PHASE\_E\_PATH\_A\_PAPER\_OUTLINE.md}~v1.0):
\textbf{(i)}~the 0/2 prior is empirical evidence about the
\emph{regime}, not just two features — both failures share a common
attribution (post-hoc literature applied to training-from-scratch) so
the prior probability of structurally-similar features (F.7 FP8, F.8
distillation, F.9 lang-cond) succeeding under the same regime is
lower than the original plan assumed;
\textbf{(ii)}~continuing under that updated prior costs $\sim$3~weeks
and $\sim$3-5~h GPU per ablation against an expected $\leq 1$ PASS
yield — marginal evidence is low, marginal cost is high;
\textbf{(iii)}~two clean failures with three-cause attributions and
four Phase~F future-work pointers is sufficient material for the C4
narrative; running three more ablations adds bulk, not structural insight;
\textbf{(iv)}~cutting losses redirects calendar to paper LaTeX, the
binding path to arXiv-and-MLSys submission.

We treat this as a \emph{successful} Phase~E2 outcome rather than a
failure of the original plan: mechanical gates committed before
experiments ran (R6) plus honest negative-result reporting (R3) is
what allowed the cut to be made on empirical evidence rather than
sunk-cost reasoning. Honest negative results plus deployable positive
contributions is the kind of paper the field needs more of at the
small-scale ternary intersection where the literature is currently
sparse.
